author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "If you suppose that you can offer sacrifice, God Himself thus speaks of you as most abandoned sinners: ‘The wicked man,’ He says, ‘that sacrificeth a calf is as if he cut off a dog’s neck; and he that offereth an oblation, as if he offered swine’s blood.’

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Recognize herein your sacrifice, who have already poured out human blood.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And again He says, ‘Their sacrifices shall be unto them as the bread of mourners; all that eat thereof shall be polluted.’"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: We say that in the case of every man the sacrifice that is offered partakes of the character of him who approaches to offer it, or approaches to partake of it; and that those eat of the sacrifices of such men, who in approaching to them partake of the character of those who offer them.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Therefore, if a bad man offer sacrifice to God, and a good man receive it at his hands, the sacrifice is to each man of such character as he himself has shown himself to be, since we find it also written that "unto the pure all things are pure."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: In accordance with this true and catholic judgment, you too are free from pollution by the sacrifice of Optatus, if you disapproved of his deeds.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For certainly his bread was the bread of mourners, seeing that all Africa was mourning under his iniquities.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But the evil involved in the schism of all your party makes this bread of mourners common to you all.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For, according to the judgment of your Council, Felicianus of Musti was a shedder of man’s blood.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For you said, in condemning them, "Their feet are swift to shed blood."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: See therefore what kind of sacrifice he offers whom you hold to be a priest, when you have yourselves convicted him of sacrilege.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And if you think that this is in no way to your prejudice, I would ask you how the emptiness of your calumnies can be to the prejudice of the whole world?
